HUMBLE ADDRESS: APPOINTMENT OF LORD MANDELSON AS HM AMBASSADOR 
TO WASHINGTON  
 
That an Humble Address be presented to His Majesty, that he will be graciously pleased to 
give directions to require the Government to lay before this House all papers relating to Lord 
Mandelson’s appointment as His Majesty’s Ambassador to the United States of America, 
including but not confined to the Cabinet Office due diligence which was passed to Number 
10, the Conflict of Interest Form Lord Mandelson provided to the Foreign, Commonwealth 
and Development Office (FCDO), material the FCDO and the Cabinet Office provided to UK 
Security Vetting about Lord Mandelson’s interests in relation to Global Counsel, including his 
work in relation to Russia and China, and his links to Jeffrey Epstein, papers for, and 
minutes of, meetings relating to the decision to appoint Lord Mandelson, electronic 
communications between the Prime Minister’s Chief of Staff and Lord Mandelson, and 
between ministers and Lord Mandelson, in the six months prior to his appointment, minutes 
of meetings between Lord Mandelson and ministers in the six months prior to his 
appointment, all information on Lord Mandelson provided to the Prime Minister prior to his 
assurance to this House on 10 September 2025 that ‘full due process was followed during 
this appointment’, electronic communications and minutes of all meetings between Lord 
Mandelson and ministers, Government officials and special advisers during his time as 
Ambassador, and the details of any payments made to Lord Mandelson on his departure as 
Ambassador and from the Civil Service except papers prejudicial to UK national security or 
international relations which shall instead be referred to the Intelligence and Security 
Committee of Parliament.

GOVERNMENT METHODOLOGY IN COMPLYING WITH THE HUMBLE ADDRESS 
 
Summary 
1.​ In order to provide transparency to Parliament and the public, below is set out the 
process by which the Government gathered, processed and published material in 
relation to the Humble Address of 4 February 2026. This includes the publication of 
Volume I on 11 March and this publication of Volume II. ​
 
2.​ The scope of the motion is very broad and has required the discovery, assessment, 
analysis and preparation of thousands of individual documents and messages. This 
is a task that has involved every Government department and goes far beyond the 
scope of any Freedom of Information Act request or Parliamentary Question. From 
the beginning it was recognised that the Government’s response would be more 
similar in scale to a public inquiry. In addition, it has been necessary for the 
Government to navigate the added complexity of an ongoing police investigation, 
which it has been consistently clear it will not prejudice in its response to the Humble 
Address. ​
 
3.​ The Government’s guiding principle in responding to the Humble Address has been 
to comply as fully and transparently as possible with the motion and Parliament’s will, 
as swiftly as possible. Other than the redaction of individual email addresses and 
contact details, the majority of redactions are those which the Government has 
agreed with the Intelligence and Security Committee of Parliament (ISC). The only 
other redactions made outside the ISC process have been where there is a clear 
rationale, informed by the FOI Act or other legal frameworks, and in line with the 
clear precedent set by previous administrations. The application of such redactions 
has been deliberately limited, targeted, and the subject of detailed consideration on a 
line-by-line basis. The reasons for redaction have been clearly marked. ​
 
4.​ The Government has invited and received comprehensive assurance at every stage 
of this process, from making the initial decisions on scope, to the minutiae of specific 
redactions. This includes advice from leading Counsel on the principles followed and 
the detailed underpinning methodology, and the Chair of the Public Administration 
and Constitutional Affairs Committee reviewing the information which has been 
withheld in order not to prejudice the ongoing Metropolitan Police Service 
investigation. ​
 
5.​ The result is the largest ever Government response to a Humble Address. It 
represents thousands of hours of work from officials across the Government to 
deliver an unprecedented piece of Government transparency.  
 
Commissioning 
 
6.​ A disclosure exercise of this size required multiple commissions to every government 
department, in order that the Government could be assured that it was discharging 
Parliament’s will. These are explained below.​

7.​ To prepare the first volume of published material, an initial set of documents relevant 
to the appointment and withdrawal of Peter Mandelson was provided to the Cabinet 
Office Humble Address team by the Prime Minister's Office, following which the 
Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office (FCDO) was commissioned to 
provide further specific documents relating to Peter Mandelson’s appointment and 
withdrawal. These documents were subject to the same series of checks as outlined 
below.​
 
8.​ In addition, all Government departments (including the Prime Minister's Office) 
received an instruction to retain all potentially relevant information shortly after the 
Humble Address motion was passed, to ensure that nothing in scope of the motion 
would be lost before departments were formally commissioned. ​
 
9.​ On 23 February, the the Senior Responsible Officer (SRO - the Permanent Secretary 
to the Cabinet Office) commissioned all departments to search for and return all 
electronic communications and minutes of meetings in scope of the Humble Address, 
in order to return information mainly within scope of the limbs of the motion 
concerned with messages and meetings during Peter Mandelson’s tenure as 
Ambassador and the six preceding months. This focused on obtaining electronic 
communications directly between Peter Mandelson and Ministers, Pay Band 3/4 
Special Advisers, Permanent Secretaries and Directors General, and minutes of 
meetings between those same individuals, where they were the principal attendees, 
rather than where they were one member among wider cast lists (for example not 
including formal committee meetings such as COBR or the NSC). This did not 
include wider groups or distribution lists, therefore excluding FCDO Diplomatic 
Telegrams, regional updates, thematic notes and briefing materials, press lines, 
corporate updates and newsletters.​
 
10.​In addition to this request, a further specific commission was made to the FCDO on 
24 February, in order to target information in scope of the limbs of the motion most 
relevant to the FCDO. This included information relating to Peter Mandelson’s 
withdrawal and severance. ​
 
11.​Departments were asked to provide assurance on their returns on 6 March, in order 
that the central Humble Address team could be sure that all relevant information had 
been collected and to understand where further commissions may be required. ​
 
12.​Departments were asked to undertake a search for any further non-corporate 
communications on 13 March, given the significant interest in this limb of the motion. 
This commission specified that all relevant non-corporate communications, except 
where they were purely personal in nature (for example relating to family matters or 
medical information), were required to be shared with the Cabinet Office. This 
included group messages.​
 
13.​A commission was sent to departments on 27 March which provided a further 
opportunity to share any additional material that departments believed should be 
considered in scope of the Humble Address motion. The commission also requested 
confirmation from departments that their initial return had captured all minutes of

meetings in scope. Such confirmation would be taken to mean that departments had 
cross-referred to specific individuals’ calendars as well as their inboxes.​
 
14.​There were two specific commissions to relevant departments to check that all 
material relating to Peter Mandelson’s National Security Vetting had been returned, 
on 21 April and 8 May. 
 
15.​Departmental Permanent Secretaries were responsible for assuring the Cabinet 
Office that they had taken reasonable steps to comply with the commissions and 
provide all relevant information. Departments were responsible for contacting all 
relevant current and former Ministers, Special Advisers and officials in their 
departments in scope of the request. Throughout the process there was an ongoing 
dialogue between the central team and departments, with specific sessions held with 
relevant private office officials to answer any questions.​
 
16.​Where individuals in scope had subsequently left the Government, the department 
which last employed them was responsible for contacting them and assuring their 
return. Where Ministers had occupied other Ministerial posts in the period between 
the six months prior to Peter Mandelson’s appointment as HMA Washington and the 
date of the Humble Address motion, the Minister’s current department was 
responsible for contacting the departments where that Minister had previously 
served.​
 
17.​The Cabinet Office Humble Address team used a secure portal to process returns 
from departments. These returns were instructed to be unredacted and undoctored.  
 
Scope of searches 
18.​In order that the Government could respond swiftly and transparently to discharge 
the spirit and letter of the Humble Address, search parameters were included based 
on the limbs of the Humble Address motion. These were as follows:​
 
a.​ Individuals in scope of the limbs of the motion seeking direct correspondence 
with Peter Mandelson (via email and other electronic messages) were 
specified as all Ministers, Pay Band 3/4 Special Advisers, Permanent 
Secretaries and Directors General. This was on the basis that these were the 
individuals most likely to have come into contact with Peter Mandelson.  
b.​ These individuals were instructed to search their work devices and personal 
devices for any relevant information. 
c.​ Minutes of meetings between those same individuals and Peter Mandelson 
where they were the principal attendees were requested, not where they were 
one member among wider cast lists (for example not including formal 
committee meetings such as the National Security Council). 
d.​ Electronic messages involving Peter Mandelson that were exchanged as part 
of a group were requested, as well as those directly between an in-scope 
individual and Peter Mandelson. 
 
19.​While commissions to departments did not specifically request invitations to meetings 
or schedulers from individuals’ calendars, where these have been provided to the

Cabinet Office Humble Address team and where relevant, they have been included in 
the publication. ​
 
20.​Some material has been included in the publication where it does not meet the above 
criteria but includes content relevant to areas where Parliament has expressed a 
significant interest, such as Peter Mandelson’s campaign to become Chancellor of 
Oxford University. Material of a party political nature is included in the publication, 
contrary to usual practice and precedent for information ‘held’ by the Government, in 
order to demonstrate the maximum possible transparency.​
 
21.​Some information was deemed out of scope via the commissioning process so as to 
keep the returns focused on the most relevant information: 
a.​ Obvious ephemera - for example press cuttings, out of office replies, FCDO 
internal corporate messaging. 
b.​ Anything which was clearly related to the corporate management of British 
Embassy Washington (e.g. FCDO personnel decisions and discussions, 
individual performance reporting, embassy staffing and management plans, 
estate issues). 
c.​ Routine Embassy business (such as consular work and copylists containing 
FCDO Diplomatic Telegrams, regional updates, thematic notes and briefing 
materials, press lines, corporate updates and newsletters). 
d.​ Purely personal information that would not usually be considered ‘held’ by the 
Government (for example relating to family matters or medical information). 
 
22.​On 31 March the SRO wrote to Peter Mandelson - via his solicitors - to request any 
information held on his personal phone. Peter Mandelson declined to comply with 
this request. The Government has no further recourse to search the personal devices 
of Peter Mandelson.  
 
Cabinet Office assessment 
 
23.​All documents submitted to the Cabinet Office underwent a series of checks 
including: 
 
●​ An assessment to determine whether the material submitted was in scope of 
the Humble Address motion.   
 
●​ A first stage assessment to determine whether material contained information 
which may prejudice UK national security or international relations. Where so, 
it was subject to a further process as outlined below.  
 
●​ A team of Civil Service reviewers conducted checks to examine whether the 
documents contained: junior officials’ names; legally privileged information; 
third party information; commercially sensitive information; or, personal data 
(e.g. phone numbers or email addresses). Such material was flagged for 
potential redaction ahead of final publication.​
 
●​ A review team of senior officials, overseen by the SRO, has made the final

decisions on scope, commissioning, and fine judgements on specific 
principles and documents. 
 
24.​As the Government has consistently set out, documents have been withheld from 
publication if they could potentially prejudice the ongoing Metropolitan Police Service 
investigation. As set out below, these are intended to be published at a later date. ​
 
National security and international relations 
 
25.​The motion required that any papers prejudicial to national security or international 
relations would be referred to the ISC. Consequently, a subset of the material above 
was subject to an additional process to comply with that aspect.   
 
26.​Although the ISC was operating under the authority of the Humble Address in 
fulfilling this task (as opposed to its normal statutory remit), the process largely 
followed the usual process for requesting and agreeing national security redactions 
(although in this case, the ISC was also agreeing to international relations redactions 
as well as those relating to national security). Departments were asked to indicate 
where their searches identified material that would be prejudicial to either national 
security or international relations (or both). Departments then prepared pro formas 
that specified the text which required redaction, and the rationale for requesting that 
redaction. The pro formas, alongside the unredacted documents, were submitted to 
the ISC to seek their approval. In undertaking this process departments were 
supported by the National Security Secretariat for national security material, and the 
FCDO for international relations material. Over 300 documents were submitted to the 
ISC to seek their approval for the Government’s proposed redactions.​
 
27.​Following the initial process, the Government and the ISC were in agreement on a 
number of the redaction decisions. The remaining documents were subject to a 
contested redactions process with a hearing held between senior officials and the 
ISC. This hearing led to the successful resolution of redactions relating to all of the 
remaining documents.  
 
28.​The ISC’s statement of 15 May makes clear that it has considered all redactions 
proposed in the documents referred to the Committee, in order not to prejudice UK 
national security or international relations. The decisions determined by the 
Committee were final following the contested redaction hearing on 14 May. The 
Government is extremely grateful to the Committee for their work. 
 
Preparation for publication 
29.​Following these processes, the Cabinet Office Humble Address team prepared the 
documents for publication. The documents include a mixture of emails, submissions, 
hard-copy files, and electronic messages such as WhatsApps, some of which 
required formatting choices to be applied for intelligibility. ​
 
30.​WhatsApp and other instant messages were transcribed in a standard format to 
ensure intelligibility. Where material such as WhatsApp messages was submitted to 
the Humble Address team with incomplete details (for instance regarding dates), the

Cabinet Office Humble Address team followed up with departments to request the 
remaining material. Material has been published with the maximum amount of 
available information, and some messages which are not strictly in scope of the 
motion have been included in order to provide context, where such context was 
necessary, or where they respond to particular areas of interest from Parliament. ​
 
31.​Documents have been de-classified to enable publication. Where documents show a 
classification marking, this no longer applies.​
  
32.​The publication is structured according to each limb of the motion. Within this, 
documents are ordered chronologically. 
 
Redactions 
33.​It is a longstanding practice across successive administrations that, when the 
Government responds to a Humble Address motion, it has a responsibility to 
consider whether it is in the public interest to place certain information into the public 
domain. In line with practice across successive administrations, this is done using the 
principles of the Freedom of Information Act, and in line with the Ministerial Code and 
the resolutions on ministerial accountability, passed by both Houses in 1997 which 
state that "ministers should be as open as possible with Parliament, refusing to 
provide information only when disclosure would not be in the public interest, which 
should be decided in accordance with relevant statute."​
 
34.​As set out above, the Government’s overriding priority in responding to the Humble 
Address has been to respond fully, transparently and swiftly in order to meet the will 
of the House. Clearly, the scale of the Humble Address disclosure goes far beyond 
any usual Freedom of Information Act or other transparency request. However, the 
principles the Freedom of Information Act sets out have been used by previous 
administrations when responding to such motions - for example when responding to 
Humble Addresses relating to Randox and the Westferry Printworks. That means 
that, outside the process for managing papers prejudicial to UK national security or 
international relations, a number of targeted, specific redactions have been made in 
order to prevent real-world harms.  
 
35.​Specifically, these targeted redactions are in order to protect: 
a.​ Individuals’ email addresses and phone numbers; 
b.​ The identities of junior civil servants; 
c.​ Third party data - including the personal or commercially sensitive data of 
third parties not relevant to the motion; and 
d.​ Legal professional privilege.​
 
36.​A small amount of Peter Mandelson’s personal data has also been redacted: his 
signature and the location of his home.​
 
37.​In the interests of transparency, third party individuals who are considered to be 
public figures have been named. Third parties who have a reasonable expectation of 
privacy, because they are not public figures, have not been named. No references to 
current or former UK politicians have been redacted on the basis of their being third

parties. Publication of the names of political figures from countries other than the 
USA has been limited to world leaders.​
 
38.​There has been particular public interest in information involving Global Counsel and 
Palantir. No redactions have been made to references to Global Counsel, other than 
to protect the privacy of individuals who are not public figures - their connection to 
Global Counsel has been made clear in those emails. References to Palantir and 
Anduril have only been redacted in cases where the redaction has been agreed with 
the ISC, i.e. on the grounds of being information that is prejudicial to UK national 
security or international relations.​
 
39.​Some company names other than Global Counsel and Palantir have been redacted 
where this is not relevant to the motion. The Government does not consider it to be in 
the interests of the taxpayer to damage the Government’s relationship with 
companies, to damage ongoing Government commercial negotiations, or to 
potentially damage those companies’ commercial prospects, if they are not relevant 
to the motion.  
 
40.​Where material that has been submitted to the Cabinet Office Humble Address team 
is already in the public domain, no redactions have been applied to that material. ​
 
41.​The redaction process has been overseen by the Cabinet Office and, where relevant, 
in conjunction with the ISC. No Government Minister or Special Adviser has 
determined any of the redactions.​
 
42.​The following labels have been used for redactions in Volume II: 
a.​ Redactions made with the agreement of the ISC have been labelled with 
asterisks ‘(***)’ 
b.​ All email addresses and phone numbers have been redacted with the label 
‘PERSONAL’ 
c.​ A small amount of personal data contained in the body of documents has also 
been redacted with the label ‘PERSONAL’ 
d.​ All junior civil servants’ names have been redacted with the label ‘JCS’ 
e.​ Third party material not relevant to the motion and relating to individuals who 
are not public figures have been redacted with the label ‘THIRD PARTY’ 
f.​
Information relating to companies, where disclosure of the information would 
be commercially damaging, has been redacted with the label ‘THIRD PARTY’ 
g.​ Material attracting legal professional privilege has been redacted with the 
label ‘LPP’ 
 
Acronyms 
43.​Below is an explanation of common acronyms used in document titles: 
a.​ CO - Cabinet Office 
b.​ DBT - Department for Business and Trade 
c.​ DEFRA - Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs 
d.​ DSIT - Department for Science, Innovation and Technology 
e.​ FCDO - Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office 
f.​
HMT - HM Treasury

g.​ HO - Home Office 
h.​ MoD - Ministry of Defence 
i.​
NCSC - National Cyber Security Centre 
j.​
No.10 - Prime Minister’s Office 
 
Independent assurance 
 
44.​The Government has taken several measures to ensure independent, external 
assurance of the process it has followed. ​
 
45.​The Senior Responsible Owner has taken advice from leading Counsel, senior 
Government lawyers, and other Government experts throughout this process.​
 
46.​As confirmed by Ministers to Parliament, the Chair of the Public Administration and 
Constitutional Affairs Committee, Simon Hoare MP, has reviewed information that 
has been withheld from publication on the basis of not wishing to prejudice the 
ongoing Metropolitan Police Service investigation, in order to provide as much 
transparency to Parliament as possible, albeit in a closed setting. ​
 
47.​Finally, the Cabinet Office Humble Address team has taken advice from an 
independent Kings Counsel - including reviewing the methodological approach 
followed by officials - and acted on that to inform their approach. This has helped 
ensure that the Government is confident that its approach is compliant with the 
Humble Address and the Government’s legal obligations. 
 
Police process and third publication 
48.​The Government has now discharged its duties under the Humble Address when it 
comes to identifying relevant material within scope of the motion and preparing it for 
publication. With the exception of the material being withheld in order not to prejudice 
the ongoing Metropolitan Police Service investigation, all these documents have now 
been published in either Volume I or II. There will be a final publication by the 
Government at the conclusion of the Metropolitan Police Service investigation, or 
before, if the Metropolitan Police Service confirms that doing so will not prejudice 
their investigation. ​

Elkie,  
Grateful if you can commission CO/UKSV to input into an Annex that will be shared 
with the FAC in response to their attached letter re. Mandelson. Grateful if we can have 
input by 2pm today please as we are aiming for FS to issue the letter today. Given 
FCDO do not own the security clearance process, the Foreign Secretary will not want 
to take responsibility for responding to the FAC on these matters. We therefore 
propose the FS write a short cover letter and an annex (owned by the Cabinet Office) 
with responses to their questions.  
To support your teams, the questions are set out below and I have included where we 
think the FCDO does have agency to answer the questions are they refer specifically 
to FCDO/the FS.  
Annex A - Cabinet Office response to the FAC questions  
1. What security concerns were raised by the agencies undertaking security
clearance ahead of Lord Mandelson’s appointment? [For CO to answer]
2. What was the response of the Foreign, Commonwealth and Development
Office to any such concerns? FCDO advice was not sought, nor given.
3. Was it the assessment of the FCDO that any such concerns represented a
potential barrier to Lord Mandelson’s appointment? FCDO advice was not
sought, nor given.
4. Were any conditions imposed on the remit of Lord Mandelson as Ambassador
in order to mitigate any security concerns? [For CO to answer]
5. Was there a decision made to dismiss security concerns? Was such a
decision taken by the FCDO or by No. 10? If so, by whom? [For CO to answer]
6. If no decision was taken to straightforwardly dismiss security concerns raised
by the relevant agencies, was a decision made to suspend or alter: (a) the
usual security vetting requirements; or, (b) the usual timeline for vetting
procedures? [For CO to answer]
7. Was it the assessment of the Foreign Secretary that there was sufficient time
between the announcement of Lord Mandelson’s appointment in December
and the beginning of his tenure on 10 February to carry out full thorough
vetting? Yes. While an expedited clearance process is faster than a routine
clearance, it is not unusual to do so, and within the bounds of the vetting
process.
8. Did the usual vetting process for diplomatic appointments commence in
relation to Lord Mandelson before his appointment was made public? [For CO
to answer]

In discussion with Foreign Office, Cabinet Office and No10 officials, I have looked into whether the 
correct process was followed for both the appointment of the previous HMA Washington, Peter 
Mandelson, and the process around your decision to withdraw him from post.

appointment from outside either the Diplomatic or Home Civil Service. You received advice from my

is now in correspondence with Lord Mandelson about the termination of his contract. [In both the

In discussion with Foreign Office, Cabinet Office and No10 officials, I have looked into whether 
the correct process was followed for both the appointment of HMA Washington, Peter 
Mandelson, and the process around your decision to withdraw him from post. As you know this 
was a political diplomatic appointment, and therefore - as set out in the note from my 
predecessor, which you received before making this appointment - requires the necessary 
security clearances and doing due diligence on any potential Conflicts of Interest or other issues 
of which you should be aware.

not carried out adequately. In my discussions with officials I have reviewed or discussed: - the 
Propriety and Ethics open source due diligence that you saw as part of the appointment process;  
- the post appointment vetting which was completed post announcement but before he took up
post; and
- your decision to withdraw HMA Washington once the content of the emails was known and
HMA Washington was given the right to reply to his line manager, Oliver Robbins (FCDO
Permanent Under Secretary)

OFFICIAL – SENSITIVE - PERSONAL 
OFFICIAL – SENSITIVE PERSONAL 
From the Cabinet Secretary 
16 September 2025 
PRIME MINISTER 
APPOINTMENT AND WITHDRAWAL FROM POST OF HMA 
WASHINGTON 
As we discussed, I have looked into whether the correct process was followed for both 
the appointment of the previous HMA Washington, Peter Mandelson, and the process 
around your decision to withdraw him from post. 
The process for his appointment was unusual but not irregular. This was a direct 
Prime Ministerial appointment from outside both the Diplomatic and Home Civil 
Service. You received advice from my predecessor on 11 November 2024 before 
deciding on this type of appointment. That advice explained your right to make such 
an appointment and the process for doing so but did not give specific advice on 
candidates.  The process that took place followed the advice my predecessor gave in 
particular “for them to acquire the necessary security clearances and do due diligence 
on any potential conflicts of interest or other issues of which you should be aware 
before confirming your choice.” All processes set out by my predecessor took place. 
Two actions, the security vetting and the process on conflicts of interest took place 
after the decision to appoint. I do not however consider that to be material as the 
vetting process was complete before the previous HMA Washington took up post on 
10 February 2025, and it is more usual for security vetting to happen after 
appointment. 
Following the advice from my predecessor, the Propriety and Ethics Team (PET) 
conducted an open-source due diligence report on Lord Mandelson. This identified his 
relationship with Jeffrey Epstein as a reputational risk.  Your special advisers then 
obtained further assurances from Lord Mandelson on this specific issue. No evidence 
has come to light suggesting concerns were raised with you or my predecessor on 
national security grounds as part of the pre-appointment timeline.  On 18 December 
2024 your office wrote to the previous Permanent Under-Secretary at the FCDO

OFFICIAL – SENSITIVE PERSONAL 
instructing him to seek The King's approval for the appointment, which was duly 
sought later the same day. 
The appointment was announced on 20 December 2024.  The FCDO immediately 
commenced the national security vetting process, submitting Lord Mandelson as a 
priority candidate for Developed Vetting to the UK Security Vetting Agency.  
Clearance was duly given before he commenced his posting on 10 February 2025.  
The FCDO also immediately took on the task of working with Lord Mandelson to 
understand and mitigate conflicts of interest in advance of his departure for 
Washington. 
Taken together this has met all of the actions set out in my predecessor’s advice of 11 
November. 
Your decision to withdraw Lord Mandelson as HMA Washington was taken in light 
of your belief that his account of his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein at the time of 
appointment had been less than totally candid, and that his replies on questions posed 
by his line manager, the FCDO Permanent Under-Secretary, left open the possibility 
that further damaging revelations would ensue. The FCDO is now in correspondence 
with Lord Mandelson about the termination of his contract. 
The evidence I have reviewed leads me to conclude that appropriate processes were 
followed in both the appointment and withdrawal of the former HMA Washington. 
I will keep this under review if any further evidence emerges. 
SIR CHRIS WORMALD KCB 
Cabinet Secretary and Head of the Civil Service

Subject: RE: Main thread - Senior Appointment
OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE
Hi 
Colleagues may have a view on terminology but I think we are ‘ appointing’ , subject to 
the conclusion of bilateral processes.
Head of Mission roles are usually advertised across Government. The FCDO's 
recruitment processes are underpinned by the principle of selection for appointment 
on merit, on the basis of fair and open competition. Recommendations for 
appointment for these roles are ratified by the Foreign Secretary and, for the most 
senior posts, the Prime Minister. Appointments are then approved by HMTK.
The Constitutional Reform and Governance (CRAG) Act 2010 is the statutory basis 
for ensuring that Civil Service recruitment is fair, open and based on merit. Section 10 
(3) of the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act allows for Head of Mission
appointments to take place outside of the CSC’ s rules and allows for political
appointments to be made to Head of Mission roles (or Governorships). Such
appointments are few in number but have been used by successive administrations
since 1945.
Best wishes
Rochelle

On behalf of Centre on Contemporary China and the World (CCCW) at
the University of Hong Kong (HKU) Director Li Cheng, I am delighted to
extend an exclusive invitation to you for a captivating guest talk with
Lord Peter Mandelson, where we will delve into the new era of British
foreign policy and its implications for Sino-UK relations and the global
landscape. The event will be available to the public for online viewing, but
the in-person event is invite-only.
Drawing from his extensive experience as a former European Trade
Commissioner and British First Secretary of State, Lord Mandelson will
provide invaluable insights on several key topics, including:
- Sir Keir Starmer's foreign policy
- The Labour Party's priorities in relation to China
- Strategies for businesses navigating geopolitical risks
- The role of higher education in fostering global cooperation
This event promises to be an enlightening experience, offering a unique
perspective from one of the foremost experts in the field. Your presence
would greatly enrich the discussion and add to the prestige of the
occasion.

Dear Sarah, 
I hope you are well and settling into your important new role. 
I am planning to reschedule the roundtable you agreed to participate in just before the 
election was called - this time focussing on how the Clean Energy Mission can be an 
important lever for building out our industrial strategy . And I wondered if you would be 
willing and available to lead off a discussion on the topic? SSE will also give some initial 
remarks. 
A number of industry people have highlighted to me that the new government's more 
ambitious decarbonisation agenda presents a major economic opportunity to onshore cable 
manufacturing, leverage the UK's geographical strengths in carbon capture and repurpose 
the UK's ports for offshore wind turbine making . The challenge will be identifying where the 
UK can compete with its international counterparts on green manufacturing and ensuring 
that policy and regulation choices develop these opportunities. In the absence of this, the UK 
risks not fully capture the benefits of decarbonisation. 
I am keen to bring together a group of interesting and, hopefully, lively people to discuss this 
opportunity and challenge. I would plan to invite a mix of industry, Labour MPs and policy 
people, senior union people and relevant influencers for what would be a Chatham House 
rule discussion.

49 - 02-09-2024 - Email between Peter Mandelson (whilst at Global Counsel) and Lord 
Patrick Vallance (Minister of State for Science, Innovation, Research and Technology)
Personal
Personal
Personal
Personal
Personal
Personal
Personal
Personal
Personal
Personal
166

The new government is now trying to do on stilts what we attempted to do 1997-10. In the two bookends of 
my ministerial career, in 1998 and 2008, we tried to make a fundamental difference to the growth and 
productivity drivers of the UK economy, primarily by changing and strengthening the supply side of the 
economy - education and skills, transport and infrastructure, university expansion and getting the right 
business and investment environment - but latterly, too, by giving the government some skin in the game in 
bringing about investment in commercialising the UK's science and technology base. 
The 1998 white paper Building a knowledge-driven economy set about replicating in Britain the west coast 
ecosystem of research/developmenUtranslation/commercialisation. It was based on principles of 
competition, collaboration and clusters of new technology-based industry around the country. 
In 2008, we created the National Economic Council which met monthly in Cobr under Gordon's 
chairmanship to galvanise everything the public and private sectors could throw at saving and regenerating 
the UK economy following the banking crisis. This was what I was brought back from Brussels to do in 
government and the white paper New industry, new jobs (Ninja) set out how to do it. 
I feel that Keir's government is trying to achieve a similar thing all over again but I am not aware of any 
granular attempt to work out how better to do so based on previous experience. You come closest to it but 
Michael Barber focused on public services not the economy (and will be relevant to other Keir missions) and 
I am not sure what Nick Boles brings. 
The truth is that we did not pull off what we attempted to do. We made a difference, undoubtedly, but did we 
bring about structural change and fundamentally transform the underlying performance of the economy? 
No. The machinery and performance of the government was not up to that job. Although I could point to a 
number of things the Treasury allowed us to take forward in my department which I agreed with Alistair 
Darling we were not really all singing from the same hymn sheet or joined up in our thinking and actions and 
we did not have scale. 
The only attempts since seem to be a mix of Greg Clark's short-lived challenges and the 'missions' 
approach you set out to the Prime Minister (Johnson ? Sunak ?) and then of course your vaccine task force. 
This was a very specific thing which Whitehall tends to do well when mobilised. I would love to think you are 
having success in making a second run but my fear is that Rachel's growth plan, Jonny's industrial strategy, 
Angela's planning and devolution reforms and DSIT's science drive are not really joined up. There are such 
big bets and trade offs to be made if wishful thinking is to be turned into meaningful, sustainable change. 
The work I have been doing for UUK on research and innovation has revealed to me that while we could 
always spend and invest more on science, we are not getting full economic value from what we are 
spending already and that UKRI, for all its strengths, hasn't the heft and punch to make the really big calls, 
notwithstanding Andrew Mackenzie's strong leadership. 
In policy and narrative, as I said to you, the government is over reliant on building houses and energy 
infrastructure as the golden route forward. These can help but there are limits to their impact on the 
economy's underlying productivity, ability to generate new businesses, establish UK supply chains and 
increase exports. 
We have started well in the UK in Al but I am less convinced we will finish sufficiently ahead. Ditto 
bioscience and quantum. The main reason is not government policy, relevant as that is, but the lack of 
really big UK private sector companies driving the necessary advance and competitiveness. Will the 
proposed Industrial Strategy Council make the transformative difference in deploying the business acumen, 
finance capital and human talent in the teams and networks of our universities and private sector ? The 
thinking so far would not suggest it. 
Labour has, let's say, two terms to succeed. So far Keir's team has not prioritised policy (not surprising given 
how much else had to be re-built after Corbyn). And the No 10 policy team is not large or experienced. Pat 
at the Cabinet Office will have the right instincts but I am an afficionado of the Cabinet Office (I started there 
as Minister without Portfolio and ended there as First Secretary of State) and it is a light touch co-ordinator 
not a power house. 
I know you will be thinking every day how to make a difference. I wish you all possible luck ! 
Very best 
Peter 
167

, 
, 
Overview:   
SoSNI had a conversation with Lord Mandelson ahead of his appointment as the UK Ambassador to the US. They 
discussed the St Patrick’s Day events in Washington, and offered an update on NI politics and the progression 
around Legacy.   
Readout: 
1. SoSNI began by congratulating Lord Mandelson and asking when he expected to be appointed, Lord Mandelson
said in 2 weeks.
2. Lord Mandelson asked about President Trump's views on NI. SoSNI said he had not spoken about NI publicly,
3. SoSNI updated Lord Mandelson on the political situation in NI, he explained this had been a very tricky time for
both Gavin Robinson and Unionism in general. He explained that there was some optimism from the Unionist
politicians around a Trump Presidency, and both Ian Paisley Jr and Baroness Hoey travelled to Washington for
the inauguration.
4.

We need 
now to supercharge our efforts to ensure that we can innovate and experiment -
with allies - at the pace of the threat. The SOR and the DIS will set out how we are 
reforming the Ministry of Defence to ensure we can get after this better. As part of 
that work, I have directed officials to work to develop options for further collaboration 
with the US, particularly arou joint testing and experimentation, and bring forward 
projects where there is the greatest shared national security and economic benefit. 
Successful implementation will be a vital 
component in driving greater innovation, speed, and industrial collaboration, 
unlocking more license-free technology and information transfer. 
In developing these proposals, we will need to carefully consider the balance 
between our partnership with the US and that with our European allies. We will also 
need to reassure stakeholders that this work is not at the expense of jobs in either 
the UK or the US but is essential to grow the sector for our shared benefit. The work 
should also be conscious of, and in alignment with, our NATO commitments, 
Lord Peter Mandelson 
HMA Washington

The Duchess of Edinburgh will be New York next week to coincide with the 69th Session 
of the UN Commission on the Status of Women (CSW). Her role as patron for the NGO 
PATH provided the original hook for the visit. It was agreed with Buckingham Palace in 
late January that HRH was permitted to add additional serials to her programme to 
support HMG objectives. These additional programme elements are all closely aligned 
to the Duchess' long-standing advocacy for work preventing Conflict Related Sexual 
Violence, and women's rights more broadly (including in other geographies, such as 
Afghanistan). The King's Principal Private Secretary has asked Royal Unit to 
provide the PUS, PPS/PM and PS/PM advice on the programme, seeking an urgent 
steer on whether the FCDO and Number 10 believe the visit should proceed (or be 
amended).
The Ukrainian programme components are 
:
•
The first is HRH speak at a meeting of the International Alliance on PSVI. The UK
created the alliance in 2023, but Ukraine is the Chair in 2025. The DPM of Ukraine will
host the meeting next week, though the Duchess’ intervention is planned to focus on her
visit to Chad, not Ukraine. FCDO officials believe this event is not contentious and hope
the Duchess is permitted to attend and speak about Chad.

We discussed – I have changed below to ensure it matches the core four points the 
PM wanted to make at PMQs in order to sound as active and confident as possible. 
Welcome views and amends to make the language more user friendly, but hopefully 
the substance makes sense. @Michael Ellam @Varun Chandra shout if anything 
goes further than you would be comfortable with on the talks.
DBT SoS statement – to be issued from Wednesday 12th March, 4am GMT
Business and Trade Secretary Jonathan Reynolds said:
“It's disappointing the US has today imposed global tariffs on steel and aluminium.
“We are taking a pragmatic approach and a trade war is in nobody’s interests
We are focused on rapidly negotiating a wider economic agreement with the US 
to eliminate additional tariffs and benefit UK business and our economy
Meanwhile we remain resolute in our support for UK industry. This Government is 
working with affected companies today, and I back industry’s application to the 
Trade Remedies Authority to investigate what further steps might be necessary to 
protect UK producers.
I will continue to engage closely and pragmatically with the US to press the case for 
UK business interests. We will keep all options on the table, and won’t hesitate to 
respond in the national interest.
ENDS
Are you going to retaliate?
The tariffs on steel and aluminium proposed by the Trump Administration are global, 
not targeted at the UK.
We are looking at all options, but we won’t be imposing tariffs immediately.
Let’s be clear. Industry doesn’t want to see a trade war where both sides keep 
escalating the situation. Standing up for industry means finding a solution.
Our approach to the US is pragmatic and we continue to have productive discussions 
on tariffs and other issues.
We have raised this with the US at various levels to make the case for UK companies 
to be exempt from US global tariffs and will continue to do so through our productive 
conversations.
You aren’t retaliating now but why aren’t you retaliating now? Other countries are.
• We are taking a pragmatic approach in seeking a wider economic deal. A trade war
is in nobody’s interests.
JCS
360

• We will keep all options on the table, but our focus is securing a deal that will benefit
industry and the British people.
Isn’t this a pretty weak response?
• I wouldn’t accept that – this is a cool-headed and pragmatic response.
• We rule nothing out and we will always act in the best interests of the British people
and economy.
• But a knee-jerk response today won’t help us achieve a long-term economic deal
with the US tomorrow, and that is our focus.
Why no exemption? PM failed at diplomacy?
We have raised this with the US at various levels to make the case for UK companies 
to be exempt from US global tariffs.
The Prime Minister has raised steel and aluminium tariffs with President Trump - in a 
call on Monday and when he was at the White House in February. The Business and 
Trade Secretary has also raised this with his counterpart Secretary Lutnick.
Our focus is on reaching a wider economic deal will benefit the industry and the 
British people.
Doesn’t say much for PM’s relationship with POTUS if they spoke and Trump ignored 
him?
• It is because of the Prime Minister’s successful visit to the White House that UK and
US officials are in talks about a future economic deal that will benefit businesses on
both sides of the Atlantic.
• These wider global tariffs have come into effect while we are in the middle of those
negotiations.
• We remain focused on that future trading partnership. We already have a fair and
balanced trade relationship and are the largest single investors in one another’s
economies, so there is plenty we can build on.
Is the industry also now going to be hit by tariffs from Trump?
The tariffs on steel, aluminium and derivatives being proposed by the Trump 
administration are global, not targeted at the UK.
We will continue to take a pragmatic approach and remain prepared to defend the 
UK's national interest where it is right to do so.
EU and Canada have announced retaliatory tariffs, weak PM?
I don’ t accept that. Our approach to the US has been pragmatic and we continue to 
have productive conversations with the US administration at all levels.
Our focus is a long-term wider economic deal which will see benefits for the industry 
361

and hardworking British people.
How will these damage the steel industry?
We have been working closely with the steel and aluminium sectors over the last few 
weeks to understand what the expected impact of tariffs will be on industry and will 
continue to do so.
In 2023, the US accounted for 5% of UK steel exports and 6% of UK aluminium 
exports.
Whilst these aren’ t huge volumes, that does not mean we are not taking this extremely 
seriously and doing everything we can to try to avoid UK industry being hit.
What are you doing to protect the steel industry?
This government will simply not allow the end of steel making in the UK.
That’ s why we have committed up to £2.5 billion of investment to rebuild the UK 
steel industry and support communities now and for generations to come. 
And next month, the final measure included in the British Industry Supercharger 
package comes into force, bringing energy costs for important UK industries –  
including steel –  closer in line with other major economies around the world.
Once fully implemented, the total value of reduced electricity prices is expected to be 
between £320 million and £410 million this year, and over £5 billion over 10 years.
We also back the industry’ s application to the Trade Remedies Authority (TRA) 
investigate what further steps might be necessary to protect UK producers.
If needed:
Only 5% of UK steel exports and 6% of aluminium exports (by volume) go to the US 
(worth noting it’ s high value, so a higher % by value).
The current steel safeguard is critical to protect our industry against unfair trading 
practices and was recently extended to June 2026.
This was designed to protect from the inflow of steel diverted from the US Market 
when it was put in place in 2018. Through the steel strategy, we will ensure that there 
is a plan for our critical steel industry following the expiration of the Safeguard.
How are we managing the risks to businesses in Northern Irelad?
We will always act in the best interests of all UK businesses which of course includes those 
in Northern Ireland.
As I have said, whilst we are not considering imposing tariffs immediately, we absolutely 
reserve the right to do so if necessary.
362

As per the Windsor Framework, where goods do not subsequently enter the EU, the Duty 
Reimbursement Scheme enables traders to reclaim or remit EU applicable duties in full 
without any limit on total claims.
We will look closely at any retaliatory tariffs announced by the EU any impact these might 
have on businesses, but it is our understanding these will not come into effect immediately 
[guide 31 March if public].
Will you revive UK tariffs on US products including whiskey, jeans and 
motorbikes?
All options are on the table so it’ s one option, but we won’ t be imposing tariffs 
immediately.
We’ re prioritising engagement with the US to stand up for industry and find a 
resolution. We will continue to take a cool-headed approach and reserve our right to 
retaliate, remaining prepared to defend the UK's national interest.
What engagement are you doing?
Industry Minister Sarah Jones met senior representatives from the steel and aluminium 
sectors yesterday and the Secretary of State speak to UK Steel today.
As you will have seen yesterday, the Trade and Economics policy manager at UK 
steel said that making the UK’ s steel exempt from the tariffs is a “ key priority”  and 
that DBT is “ all over it” .
Some steel companies have said you should make preparations to implement 
emergency tariffs should there be evidence of import surges in the coming 
months. Are you?
UK Steel has applied to the TRA for a functional review of the steel safeguard 
measure in light of the US tariffs, as well as broader challenges posed by global 
overcapacity in the steel market, and I [the Business and Trade Secretary] back that 
application.
Market reaction?
As you know we do not comment on markets.
How are we managing the risks to businesses in Northern Irelad?
We will always act in the best interests of all UK businesses which of course includes 
those in Northern Ireland.
As I have said, whilst we are not considering imposing tariffs immediately, we 
absolutely reserve the right to do so if necessary.
As per the Windsor Framework, where goods do not subsequently enter the EU, the 
Duty Reimbursement Scheme enables traders to reclaim or remit EU applicable duties 
363

in full without any limit on total claims.
We will look closely at any retaliatory tariffs announced by the EU any impact these 
might have on businesses, but it is our understanding these will not come into effect 
immediately [guide 31 March if public].
What trade protections are in place for the steel and aluminium industries?
The UK currently applies 44 trade remedy measures in addition to the globally applied 
steel safeguard measure.
We currently apply a global safeguard measure on 14 separate steel product 
categories, 17 separate anti-dumping or anti-subsidy measures on certain steel imports, 
and 2 measures on certain aluminium imports.  
The Trade Remedies Authority regularly discusses trade remedy measures with 
industry, and we encourage continued dialogue and any applications for new measures 
or the review of existing measures to ensure that UK businesses are protected from 
unfair foreign trade practices.
Next steps to securing a trade deal? Timelines?
Getting the right deal is the most important thing, but we are working at pace with the 
US to deliver this.
We are already having regular dialogue with each other.
Why are you waiting until Spring to publish the Steel Strategy when industry 
needs action now?
We have committed to publish Our Plan for Steel, on which we’re currently 
consulting, in the Spring. It will look at long-term issues facing the sector such as high 
electricity costs, unfair trading practices and scrap metal recycling.
We want to move swiftly, but in a way which addresses the longstanding issues the 
sector has faced for years and in line with other key workstreams such as developing 
the Industrial Strategy and Trade Strategy.
This is not easy, but we are determined to succeed.
OFFICIAL

US TARIFFS 20250312
ISSUE: The US global tariffs on steel and aluminium have come into effect.
RESPONSE:
It is disappointing to see the US has imposed global tariffs on steel and aluminium.
We absolutely reserve the right to impose retaliatory tariffs, and we will continue to 
have productive conversations with the US administration at all levels on this issue.
But our focus is on reaching a wider economic deal will benefit the industry and the 
British people.
Any action we take will be in the interest of UK businesses, consumers and the 
economy.
Response to tariffs?
The tariffs on steel and aluminium proposed by the Trump Administration are global, 
not targeted at the UK.
We are looking at all options, but we won’ t be imposing tariffs immediately.
Let’ s be clear. Industry doesn’ t want to see a trade war where both sides keep 
escalating the situation. Standing up for industry means finding a solution.
Our approach to the US is pragmatic and we continue to have productive discussions 
370

on tariffs and other issues.
We have raised this with the US at various levels to make the case for UK companies 
to be exempt from US global tariffs and will continue to do so through our productive 
conversations.
Why no exemption? PM failed at diplomacy?
We have raised this with the US at various levels to make the case for UK companies 
to be exempt from US global tariffs.
The Prime Minister has raised steel and aluminium tariffs with President Trump - in a 
call on Monday and when he was at the White House in February. The Business and 
Trade Secretary has also raised this with his counterpart Secretary Lutnick.
Our focus is on reaching a wider economic deal will benefit the industry and the 
British people.
Is the industry also now going to be hit by tariffs from Trump?
The tariffs on steel, aluminium and derivatives being proposed by the Trump 
administration are global, not targeted at the UK.
We will continue to take a pragmatic approach and remain prepared to defend the 
UK's national interest where it is right to do so.

15 March 2025 
The Rt. Hon Jonathan Reynolds MP 
Secretary of State for Business and Trade 
Dear Jonny, 
Scenesetter: your visit to Washington DC, 17-18th March 
Your visit will demonstrate UK commitment to advancing the Prime Minister and President’ s 
economic and technology deal. It comes at a critical time after US tariffs on steel and aluminium and 
ahead of expected aggressive “reciprocal” tariffs on 2 April. Sectoral tariffs, e.g. on pharmaceuticals, 
semi-conductors, autos, are also coming down the track. 
This is not just about “equalising” America’ s trade relationships through addressing tariffs and non-
tariff barriers. The America First policy aims to rebuild domestic manufacturing and create a new 
global trade order with allies working closely together (following US rules) that leaves China 
increasingly on the “other side of the fence”.
From the Ambassador  
The Rt Hon Lord Mandelson PC 
His Majesty’ s Ambassador 
3100 Massachusetts Ave, NW 
Washington, DC  20008 
Tel:  
132 - 15-03-2025 - Letter from Peter Mandelson to Jonathan Reynolds 
(former Secretary of State, DBT)

You and officials will be ready to present a clear and detailed offer on Tuesday. There are high 
expectations that the UK is coming with a serious proposal. You will need to speak to the full set of 
issues they care about as a collective 
It is great you are also seeing business groups and key CEOs. Congress is in recess, after reaching a 
deal on the US budget to avert a shutdown.  
I will be sorry to miss you, but I am committed to events on the West Coast with big tech. I look forward 
to hearing how the talks have gone and wish you all the best in what is a crucial endeavour for our 
country.  
Lord Mandelson

***
***
390

undertake.
I will join many of Britain’ s greatest AI innovators and investors this week in California 
for the Global Tech Conference, the world’ s premier AI gathering. Of the many 
issues we will discuss with our US and allied counterparts, I expect a central theme to 
dominate: the strategic technology rivalry between us and our adversaries.
Once more, we must combine our strengths to keep our citizens ahead in the global 
race. Our two science-rich nations, the US and UK, are the only two western countries 
with trillion-dollar technology eco-systems. Our combined research and innovation 
prowess - the US and UK are home to the top fifteen universities in the world - 
arguably remains our greatest defence against the values of freedom we cherish.
The challenges we face today are as profound as any we have confronted together in 
our past. Yet so are the opportunities.
While many governments are focussed on the risks and the dangers of AI’ s unknown 
powers, too few are investing enough energy to unlocking the enormity of life 
improving potential AI can unleash, from health, to education, to the security of our 
people.
And as with Manhattan and Polaris, together the US and UK are natural allies to work 
together to deliver generational advances. Our two governments should steer, 
facilitate and enable our world-leading industries and scientists to deliver bigger 
outcomes offered by advocacy in technology.
We are at a genuine inflection point. Now is the moment to back the innovators at the 
tech frontier. Instead of over-regulating these new technologies, our two governments 
stand ready to enable and seize the almost unimaginable benefits that they offer. The 
fair exchange of manufactured products is, and will of course remain, important to 
both US and UK economies. But the trade agreement Prime Minster Starmer and 
President Trump left the West Wing seeking a few weeks ago imagines a much 
broader and future-focussed collaboration that looks well beyond goods and services, 
to a full spectrum technology partnership between Britain and America.
I am excited by the breakthroughs such a partnership could accelerate together. In 
Biotech, there is huge potential in genomics, protein design and engineering. Artificial 
Intelligence could enable earlier and more precise diagnosis, better drug discovery 
and personalised cancer vaccines. We are determined to build advanced data centres 
and develop the world’ s first advanced quantum computer. And there are 
opportunities in collaborating to advance nuclear technology. These are just a few 
areas in which the US and UK could create moonshot achievements. Our vision for 
this partnership should span a range of possibilities, on earth and in space.
In an era of heightened competition and contest, we cannot afford to stand still. It is 
vital that our great democracies adapt to counter new threats, retain the edge over our 
adversaries and embrace new opportunities across critical advanced technologies to 
improve the daily lives of all of our citizens. As closely allied democracies, America 
and Britian can achieve more together than they can do apart. This is why a 
technology partnership needs to be at the heart of any US-UK economic deal. We 
need to think MEGA –  Make our Economies Great Again –  for all our prosperity and 
security.
ENDS

3. The Greens succeeded, however, in extracting significant amendments to the
proposed €500bn infrastructure fund:
• The extra-budgetary fund of €500bn, previously negotiated between the CDU and
SPD, was originally vaguely earmarked for infrastructure of which €100bn to be
allocated to the German states. Following negotiations with the Greens, the fund will
now also allocate €100bn to climate action (contributing to the goal of reaching climate
neutrality by 2045). The €500bn will be additional to regular spending: it must fund new
investment, not plug gaps in existing budgets.
• The regular budget must now reach an investment quota of 10% before investments
from the special fund are tapped. This Greens proposal ensures additionality by making
the ability to draw down the €500bn dependent on substantial infrastructure spend in the
regular budget.
• The definition of defence has been expanded to include debt for civil protection,
intelligence services, aiding countries who are being attacked in contravention of
international law (Ukraine) and information security. The inclusion of Ukraine spending
is a significant since we understand this to mean that German military, humanitarian and
financial support to Ukraine would now be exempt from the debt brake.
Next steps
4. A vote in the Bundestag is scheduled for Tuesday, followed on Friday 21 March by a
vote in the Bundesrat (where representatives from the German states sit). To pass
through the Bundesrat, votes from Bavaria (and therefore support of the Free Voters
party) will be needed. The Free Voters (a localised party, particularly focused on farming
issues in Bavaria) are currently opposed, but hopes are that they will be brought round
by Bavaria's Christian Social Union.
Reactions
5. Merz has said that they have come up with a package that they can put to a vote
"with a clear conscience", while Lars Klingbeil (Leader of the SPD) noted: "We in the
political centre have laid the foundation for Germany to get back on its feet and to
defend itself".
6. Various economists have been central in advising the government on the fiscal
package. Clemens Fuest, Director of the Munich-based Institute for Economic
Research, welcomed the agreement reached on additionality, arguing that the
infrastructure fund should not result in less money from core budgets being spent on
infrastructure. However, economists also warn of possible risks, including raised
borrowing costs, concerns on effectiveness of spending in defence and infra, as well as
inflationary risks.
 The €500bn infrastructure
investment fund amounts to a stimulus of around 1% of GDP each year for the next ten
years: comparable to the impact on the German economy of the Marshall Plan or
reconstruction of Eastern Germany (1.0% of GDP). Market reaction last week was
strong in relation to both the DAX, Euro and Bund yields. And all of this change should
still keep the German debt/GDP ratio below 85%.

Summary: CDU, SPD and Greens reach agreement on the defence and infrastructure fiscal 
package announced by Merz last Tuesday (see Diptel 
). This secures the 2/3 
Bundestag majority required for the changes. Greens extract concessions from CDU and 
SPD that will extend the scale of the stimulus. The package still requires approval by the 
Bundesrat.
Detail
1. On Friday, Friedrich Merz announced the outcome of negotiations between the
Greens, SPD and CDU/CSU. Changes to the original proposal were required to secure
Greens approval and confirm that a revolution in German fiscal policy is underway.
2. All three parties affirmed their support for defence spending above 1% to be exempt
from the German debt brake, which limits borrowing to 0.35% of GDP.
3. The Greens succeeded, however, in extracting significant amendments to the
proposed €500bn infrastructure fund:
" The extra-budgetary fund of €500bn, previously negotiated between the CDU and 
SPD, was originally vaguely earmarked for infrastructure of which €100bn to be 
allocated to the German states. Following negotiations with the Greens, the fund 
will now also allocate €100bn to climate action (contributing to the goal of reaching 
climate neutrality by 2045). The €500bn will be additional to regular spending: it 
must fund new investment, not plug gaps in existing budgets.
" The regular budget must now reach an investment quota of 10% before investments 
from the special fund are tapped. This Greens proposal ensures additionality by 
making the ability to draw down the €500bn dependent on substantial 
infrastructure spend in the regular budget.
" The definition of defence has been expanded to include debt for civil protection, 
intelligence services, aiding countries who are being attacked in contravention of 
international law (Ukraine) and information security. The inclusion of Ukraine 
spending is a significant since we understand this to mean that German military, 
humanitarian and financial support to Ukraine would now be exempt from the debt 
brake.
Next steps
4. A vote in the Bundestag is scheduled for Tuesday, followed on Friday 21 March by a
vote in the Bundesrat (where representatives from the German states sit). To pass
through the Bundesrat, votes from Bavaria (and therefore support of the Free Voters
party) will be needed. The Free Voters (a localised party, particularly focused on farming
issues in Bavaria) are currently opposed, but hopes are that they will be brought round
by Bavaria's Christian Social Union.
Reactions
5. Merz has said that they have come up with a package that they can put to a vote
"with a clear conscience", while Lars Klingbeil (Leader of the SPD) noted: "We in the
political centre have laid the foundation for Germany to get back on its feet and to
defend itself".
6. Various economists have been central in advising the government on the fiscal
package. Clemens Fuest, Director of the Munich-based Institute for Economic
Research, welcomed the agreement reached on additionality, arguing that the
infrastructure fund should not result in less money from core budgets being spent on

years: comparable to the impact on the German economy of the Marshall Plan or
reconstruction of Eastern Germany (1.0% of GDP). Market reaction last week was
strong in relation to both the DAX, Euro and Bund yields. And all of this change should
still keep the German debt/GDP ratio below 85%.

Well, the one thing I will say that no one should listen to anyone in the markets talk about the US 
dollar, other than President Trump or myself. We are the only ones who speak for this administration, 
the United States Government on dollar policy. I didn't tell you we have a strong dollar policy, and 
we're putting in all of the necessary ingredients to make sure the dollar is strong over the long run. 
Can it go up and down on a Bloomberg terminal over the short run? Sure? Can individual bilateral 
moves happen between the dollar and the Mexican currency, the dollar in the yen. Sure, that's that's 
what I made my living going, I'm glad it doesn't. But over time, if we put in solid economic 
506

fundamentals the and do this transformative program, then the dollar is going to do great, and the 
American people are going to debris. 
Tucker Carlson   
Secretary, thank you very much. Thank you. 
Scott Bessent 
Appreciate it.

White House Deputy Chief of Staff, Stephen Miller, did a Fox News 
broadcast interview with Sandra Smith. During the conversation Miller 
defended President Trump’ s new tariff’ s and the need for them “ The 
United States is 36% of global consumption, and we're more than 25% of 
the world's economy, just the United States alone. That means we get to 
set the rules and terms for international trade”  He also referenced 
Europe several times:
Stephen Miller
Our leaders allowed foreign countries to rig the rules of the game, to
cheat, to steal, to rob, to plunder, that has cost America trillions of dollars
in wealth. These countries cannot win a trade fight with us, because
they're already the ones who have cheated. So in other words, they've
stolen our industries. We have all the upside here in this fight, because we
can now make these industries come back, because we have the market.
We have the consumers they need. Japan cannot replace the American
automobile market. Europe can't replace the American automobile 
market.
They need us. And this is a fundamental issue of fairness and national
security.”
“We protect and defend all of Europe, and it costs them trillions of dollars
over time. And what do they do? They run an over $300 million trade
deficit with the United States. Why should America protect the world, send
foreign aid to the world, defend the world, provide for the world, in
exchange get ripped off by every other country in the world.”
Please find a rough transcript below:
Sandra Smith
All right. Fox news alert here, as the selling does seem to continue in the 
American stock market, you've got the Dow sinking to the lows of the 
session as we speak right now, down 2100, points at 38,418. Okay, so 
let's bring in Stephen Miller. He's at the White House. He's the White 
House Deputy Chief of Staff. Your timing is perfect, because I'm sure 
there are a lot of folks out there who would like to know the White House 
thinking here, Stephen, please do share with us your thoughts in this 
moment, and especially after China announced these retaliatory tariffs, it 
seems like that's when the markets took another leg down here. Where is 
all of this going next?
Stephen Miller
Well, let's first establish an important foundation. The United States is 36% 
of global consumption, and we're more than 25% of the world's economy, 
just the United States alone. That means we get to set the rules and terms 
for international trade. But we've done the opposite as a country for 
decades now, when we hear the term globalization, what does that really 
mean? It means taking America's wealth and shipping it to foreign 
510

countries. So all the factories that used to be in America are now in China, 
Cambodia, Thailand, Vietnam, Canada, Mexico, parts of Africa, India, 
Europe, Japan, Korea, you know where they’ re not? the United States of 
America. Because our leaders allowed foreign countries to rig the rules of 
the game, to cheat, to steal, to rob, to plunder, that has cost America 
trillions of dollars in wealth. These countries cannot win a trade fight with 
us, because they're already the ones who have cheated. So in other 
words, they've stolen our industries. We have all the upside here in this 
fight, because we can now make these industries come back, because we 
have the market. We have the consumers they need. Japan cannot 
replace the American automobile market. Europe can't replace the 
American automobile market. They need us. And this is a fundamental 
issue of fairness and national security. Tell me. Why is it that if you go to 
Tokyo, there's no American cars if you go to Berlin, there's no American 
cars, but on our streets we have every foreign car there is in the world. 
They've locked their markets to our cars, they have stolen from our 
workers and our people, and President Trump is saying that has to end. 
We need our wealth back and our jobs back and the quality of America, 
the American quality of life will rise exponentially as these policies kick in, 
and as the great onshoring and return of American wealth occurs. 
Sandra Smith
Are you confident we can replace that production, and we can do so in a 
way that Americans can actually afford, and like those cars?
Stephen Miller
without question. In fact, the changes in advanced manufacturing 
technology, 3d printing, robotics, artificial intelligence. This makes it more 
and more affordable to manufacture, produce and build at scale in the 
United States. American workers should not have to compete with child 
slave wages in Vietnam. They should not have to. That is wrong. We do 
not need a global race to the bottom that says that American workers have 
to earn the same wages and work in the same conditions as the poorest 
workers in the third world. That's the system that we've had with the right 
tax policy, the right regulation policy, the right trade policy, and investing in 
advanced manufacturing, all of these industries and supply chains can 
come back home. But here's the thing, it's not a choice. It's a national 
security imperative. What would happen if there was a war and we didn't 
have the ability to manufacture our own steel, our own aluminum, our own 
medicine, our own automobiles, our own aircraft? What would happen to 
the United States of America? It is a fundamental matter of national 
survival.
Sandra Smith
Yeah, well, we got a glimpse of that during COVID right when people 
couldn't get what they needed. We learned so much of it was being made 
outside of the United States. And how about the food industry? This was 
really interesting perspective right here on America Reports last hour from 
a shrimper. Listen.
Craig Reave (Sea Eagle Seafood Owner)
511

we've been getting killed by imports for not just years, but literally 
decades. So we've been suffering for a long, long time, and these tariffs, 
we believe are going to give us some immediate relief. We've outsourced 
our whole industry. 94% of the shrimp consumed in the United States is 
imported.
Sandra Smith
Okay so he shared with us, he's optimistic that this could help his industry 
survive, and they've seen a decline for decades now, because all of these, 
you know, all the shrimps been coming in from all these other countries 
into the United States, low quality shrimp, he pointed out, but there's. 
Stephen Miller
By the way, a lot of that, a lot of the seafood that we're importing has 
formaldehyde in it, is not safe for consumption, and this is this whole 
situation. People have to understand just how badly we've been ripped off 
as a country. You heard that gentleman, 94% of ship is imported to the 
United States, and we have the Gulf of America. The US agriculture and 
seafood industry has been decimated because we allow for subpar, cheap 
foreign imports without limit that are subsidized by foreign countries to put 
our farmers and our shrimpers out of business. Well, their markets are 
close to ours. Can someone explain to me, we protect and defend South 
Korea, and in exchange, they steal our automobile industry, they steal our 
electronics industry. We protect and defend Japan, and exchange they 
close their market to our cars. We protect and defend Canada, and they 
run up a massive trade deficit with us. We protect and defend all of 
Europe, and it costs them trillions of dollars over time. And what do they 
do? They run an over $300 million trade deficit with the United States. 
Why should America protect the world, send foreign aid to the world, 
defend the world, provide for the world, in exchange get ripped off by 
every other country in the world. What we have to show for it, the 
deindustrialization of the heartland, the destruction of the American 
Dream, the eradication of our industrial and manufacturing base that we 
need for national security and the crushing of the American dream. It has 
to stop, not to mention $36 trillion in debt.
Sandra Smith
I’ ve only got a few seconds left, but people are throwing around the R 
word, and they see the Dow down 2100 points. And people are worried 
about a recession. They're worried that we can't do this without sending 
this economy into such a deep dive that we can't come back from it. And I 
know you guys are all about growth. I know you're about lowering taxes, 
rolling back regulations, and seeing that growth. Are you concerned that 
this is going to put us into a recession?
Stephen Miller
The exact opposite. We inherited from Joe Biden, an economy in total free 
fall. There has been zero jobs growth for American workers in four years. 
In other words, all jobs growth had gone to foreign workers. That is a 
depression, not a recession. That is a depression for the American worker. 
512

We had debt and deficits that were rising unsustainably that would lead us 
into a certain debt and deficit crisis. We had inflation that was in high 
double digits, crushing the American family, making it impossible to afford 
the essentials of life. We had an economy that was in a state of calamity 
and catastrophe. President Trump's policy of reshoring our supply chains, 
pushing for total expensing of capital gains, eliminating regulation, 
eliminating intrusive green, new scam environmental rules, pushing for the 
biggest, broadest tax cut in American history, and reducing taxes on 
American workers, including ending taxes on tips, ending taxes on 
overtime, ending taxes on Social Security. This economic program is the 
single most important program for American wealth, prosperity and 
rejuvenation that we've ever seen in this country.
Sandra Smith
I’d love to continue the conversation hope to have you back on soon 
Steven, thank you so much.
Stephen Miller
Thank you

NCA fits in, being the national lead on SOC and setting the strategic direction across 
Policing; you also covered his concern about possible tensions. 
Data Access / online activity / combating the use of data to facilitate crime was on 
HMA’s mind.  You explained the change we have seen in criminality and how it has 
moved ‘online’, particularly fraud, cybercrime and CSAE, but you also pointed out 
how almost all other crime has a data angle, due to phone/device usage. 
You spoke in detail about the UKUSDAA 
. At HMA’s request, you explained the 
DAA and E2EE to him in detail,  highlighting the difference between lawful access 
and content moderation, but how both were impacted by E2EE.  You informed HMA 
about our ILO in NCMEC and how NCMEC operates - millions of reports from tech 
companies being triaged via NCMEC, resulting in global referrals. You informed HMA 
that these referrals lead to 800 arrests per month and 1000 children being 
safeguarded as a result. HMA was visibly shocked by the figures you quoted. You 
went on to explain that we estimate that between 1.3 and 1.6% of adults in the UK 
(approximately 800,000) pose a serious sexual risk to children and how tech 
companies signpost us to this material, for us to act upon, hopefully preventing 
contact abuse. 
AP 1: You suggested HMA visited NCMEC, which he was extremely keen to do, and 
I agreed to arrange (16/4 update: visit confirmed for 13/5).  
HMA enquired about your ‘general’ counterparts, and you spoke about the FBI, DEA, 
HSI & USSS. You talked about the opportunities for collaboration even with the 
change in priority to drugs/OIC on the SW border. You spoke about cyber and online 
crimes, detailing our tremendous success with the use of US sanctions tools to 
tackle Russian ransomware attacks. 
 
HMA was due to meet with META that afternoon and sought your views.  You 
discussed how valuable they were particularly in relation to fraud and that META was 
the biggest contributor to NCMEC cyber tips, but you expressed concern about the 
impact of E2EE. 
 
***

our footprint in the USA and how our Miami office also covers the Caribbean – 
 
Overall, a very positive first meeting, with HMA’s interest and engagement increasing 
as he learned more about the NCA and our mission, setting the tone for additional 
engagement at post. 
Thanks,
